input.query_abstract: We review theoretical approaches to the understanding of food webs. After an overview of the available food web data, we discuss three different classes of models. The first class comprise static models, which assign links between species according to some simple rule. The second class are dynamical models, which include the population dynamics of several interacting species. We focus on the question of the stability of such webs. The third class are species assembly models and evolutionary models, which build webs starting from a few species by adding new species through a process of "invasion" (assembly models) or "speciation" (evolutionary models). Evolutionary models are found to be capable of building large stable webs.
input.hyp_abstract: Theoretical models of gene regulatory circuitry are reviewed. After summarizing genome-wide expression compendia and inferred transcription-factor interaction maps, we distinguish three classes of model. The first class are static ensemble models, which wire activating and repressing regulatory edges among genes by simple probabilistic rules and study the resulting connectivity statistics. The second class are dynamical models, in which coupled ordinary differential equations govern the abundances of transcripts and proteins across interacting loci; we emphasize the robustness of steady expression states, deriving conditions under which a fixed point of the regulatory system is asymptotically stable from the eigenvalues of the linearized dynamics. We highlight that richly interconnected circuits are not automatically stable and that specific motif structure is required to reconcile high connectivity with dynamical robustness. The third class are growth models, in which the network is enlarged over evolutionary time by duplication of existing loci followed by divergence of their regulatory inputs, with a selection step retaining only variants that preserve viable expression programs. We find that such duplication-and-divergence assembly can construct large, homeostatically stable regulatory architectures displaying the modularity and degree heterogeneity seen in real organisms, whereas equivalent randomly rewired circuits are typically dynamically fragile. We conclude by discussing how mutational network growth shapes the evolvability of developmental control.
input.candidate_title: Reciprocity of Networks with Degree Correlations and Arbitrary Degree Sequences
input.candidate_abstract: Although most of the real networks contain a mixture of directed and bidirectional (reciprocal) connections, the reciprocity $r$ has received little attention as a subject of theoretical understanding. We study the expected reciprocity of networks with an arbitrary degree sequence and a broad class of degree correlations by means of statistical ensemble approach. We demonstrate that degree correlations are crucial to understand the reciprocity in real networks and a hierarchy of correlation contributions to $r$ is revealed. Numerical experiments using novel network randomization methods show very good agreement to our analytical estimations.
